title: Understand: thermodynamics
description: Check your understanding of thermodynamics in this set of free practice questions aligned to NGSS standards.

assessment_items.question_content:
A family is packing for a picnic. They put two $0\degree\text{C}$ cold packs, several $27\degree\text{C}$ cans of soda, and two $10\degree\text{C}$ containers of salad from the refrigerator into an insulated bag.

**Which of the following statements correctly describes how thermal energy will be transferred?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Thermal energy is transferred from the higher temperature objects to the lower temperature objects.
In this case, the containers of salad and the soda cans are both at higher temperatures than the cold packs.
So, the containers of salad and the soda cans transfer thermal energy to the cold packs.

choices.content: The containers of salad and the soda cans transfer thermal energy to the cold packs.
The containers of salad transfer thermal energy to the soda cans and the cold packs.
The cold packs transfer thermal energy to the containers of salad and the soda cans.
The cold packs and the soda cans transfer thermal energy to the containers of salad.

assessment_items.question_content:
A group of students are going to have a picnic. They put two $45\degree\text{F}$ bottles of water, two $80\degree\text{F}$ sandwich containers, and several $55\degree\text{F}$ cans of soda into an insulated lunch bag.

**Which of the following statements correctly describe how thermal energy will be transferred?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Thermal energy is transferred from the higher temperature objects to the lower temperature objects.
In this case, the sandwich containers and the soda cans are both at higher temperatures than the water bottles.
So, the sandwich containers and the soda cans transfer thermal energy to the water bottles.

choices.content: The soda cans and the sandwich containers transfer thermal energy to the water bottles.
The soda cans transfer thermal energy to the sandwich containers and the water bottles.
The water bottles transfer thermal energy to the sandwich containers and the soda cans.
The water bottles and the soda cans transfer thermal energy to the sandwich containers.

assessment_items.question_content:
A group of friends are going to a barbecue. They put a $20\degree\text{F}$ cold pack, a $40\degree\text{F}$ bottle of lemonade, and some $70\degree\text{F}$ cans of soda into a cooler.

**Which of the following statements correctly describe how thermal energy will be transferred?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Thermal energy is transferred from the higher temperature objects to the lower temperature objects.
In this case, the bottle of lemonade and the soda cans are both at higher temperatures than the cold packs.
So, the bottle of lemonade and soda cans transfer thermal energy to the cold packs.

choices.content: The bottle of lemonade and the soda cans transfer thermal energy to the cold pack.
The bottle of lemonade transfers thermal energy to the soda cans and the cold pack.
The cold pack transfers thermal energy to the bottle of lemonade and the soda cans.
The cold pack and the bottle of lemonade transfer thermal energy to the soda cans.

assessment_items.question_content:
Joiya grabs a cold bottle of water from the refrigerator and carries it across her dining room. The dining room is at room temperature.

**Which of the following describe possible ways heat would be transferred in this system?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: If objects are in thermal contact, heat will spontaneously transfer from warmer objects to cooler objects.
In this case, Joiya is warmer than the room-temperature air. The bottle of water is cooler than both Joiya and the air.
So, heat transfers from Joiya to the bottle of water and from the air to the bottle of water.

choices.content: Heat transfers from Joiya to the bottle of water.
Heat transfers from the air to Joiya.
Heat transfers from the bottle of water to the air.
Heat transfers from the air to the bottle of water.

assessment_items.question_content:
Rudra pours hot tea into a cup for his breakfast and places the cup on a table in the kitchen. 

**Which of the following describe possible ways heat would be transferred in this system?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: If objects are in thermal contact, heat will spontaneously transfer from warmer objects to cooler objects.
In this case, the tea is the warmest substance, and because the china cup is being warmed by the tea, the china cup is warmer than the air.
So, heat transfers from the tea to the china cup and to the air. Heat also moves from the china cup to the air.

choices.content: Heat transfers from the tea to the china cup.
Heat transfers from the china cup to the air.
Heat transfers from the tea to the air.
Heat transfers from the air to the china cup.

assessment_items.question_content:
Sammie has a pile of identical stepping stones that have been warmed in the sun on a hot day. She places three stones in a cold stream to create a path for crossing. The stones are not touching each other.   

**Which of the following describe possible ways heat would be transferred in this system?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: If objects are in thermal contact, heat will spontaneously transfer from warmer objects to cooler objects.
In this case, the stones are warmer than the air. The water in the stream is cooler than both the stones and the air.
So, heat transfers from the stones to the water in the stream. Heat also transfers from the air to the water in the stream.

choices.content: Heat transfers from the stones to the water in the stream.
Heat transfers from the water in the stream to the stones.
Heat transfers between the stones.
Heat transfers from the air to the water in the stream.

assessment_items.question_content:
A full bottle containing $2\text{ kg}$ of water at $25\degree\text{C}$ and another bottle containing $1\text{ kg}$ of water at an unknown temperature are placed in an insulated bag. Upon reaching thermal equilibrium, the full bottle’s temperature decreases to $15\degree\text{C}$. The specific heat of water is $4.18\,\dfrac{\text{kJ}}{\text{kg}\degree\text{C}}$. Assume the system is thermally isolated.

**Which of the following statements is correct?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: To solve this problem, we need to calculate the change in thermal energy. To do this, we can use the formula $Q = mC( T_{\text{f}} -T_{\text{i}} )$, where $m$ is the mass of the object, $C$ is its specific heat capacity, $T_\text{f}$ and $T_\text{i}$ are its final and initial temperatures respectively, and $Q$ is the change in thermal energy.

We don’t know the initial temperature of the other bottle. But since the system is thermally isolated, the total thermal energy of the system does not change. Therefore, the energy that the full bottle loses is transferred to the $1\text{ kg}$ bottle. So, the $1\text{ kg}$ bottle must have gained $83.6\text{ kJ}$.
So, the full bottle lost $83.6\text{ kJ}$ of thermal energy, and the other bottle gained $83.6\text{ kJ}$ of thermal energy.

choices.content: The full bottle lost $83.6\text{ kJ}$ of thermal energy, and the other bottle gained $83.6\text{ kJ}$ of thermal energy.
The full bottle lost $83.6\text{ kJ}$ of thermal energy, and the other bottle gained $41.8\text{ kJ}$ of thermal energy.
The full bottle gained $83.6\text{ kJ}$ of thermal energy, and the other bottle lost $83.6\text{ kJ}$ of thermal energy.
The full bottle lost $41.8\text{ kJ}$ of thermal energy, and the other bottle gained $41.8\text{ kJ}$ of thermal energy.

assessment_items.question_content:
A full bottle containing $2\text{ kg}$ of water at $10\degree\text{C}$ and another bottle containing $1\text{ kg}$ of water at an unknown temperature are placed in an insulated bag. Upon reaching thermal equilibrium, the full bottle’s temperature increases to $15\degree\text{C}$. The specific heat of water is $4.18\,\dfrac{\text{kJ}}{\text{kg}\degree\text{C}}$. Assume the system is thermally isolated.

**Which of the following statements is correct?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: To solve this problem, we need to calculate the change in thermal energy. To do this, we can use the formula $Q = mC( T_{\text{f}} -T_{\text{i}} )$, where $m$ is the mass of the object, $C$ is its specific heat capacity, $T_\text{f}$ and $T_\text{i}$ are its final and initial temperatures respectively, and $Q$ is the change in thermal energy.

We don’t know the initial temperature of the other bottle. But since the system is thermally isolated, the total thermal energy of the system does not change. Therefore, the energy that the full bottle gained was transferred from the $1\text{ kg}$ bottle. So, the $1\text{ kg}$ bottle must have lost $41.8\text{ kJ}$.
So, the full bottle gained $41.8\text{ kJ}$ of thermal energy, and the other bottle lost $41.8\text{ kJ}$ of thermal energy.

choices.content: The full bottle lost $41.8\text{ kJ}$ of thermal energy, and the other bottle gained $41.8\text{ kJ}$ of thermal energy.
The full bottle gained $41.8\text{ kJ}$ of thermal energy, and the other bottle lost $20.9\text{ kJ}$ of thermal energy.
The full bottle gained $41.8\text{ kJ}$ of thermal energy, and the other bottle lost $41.8\text{ kJ}$ of thermal energy.
The full bottle lost $41.8\text{ kJ}$ of thermal energy, and the other bottle gained $20.9\text{ kJ}$ of thermal energy.

assessment_items.question_content:
A half-full bottle containing $1\text{ kg}$ of water at $5\degree\text{C}$ and full bottle containing $2\text{ kg}$ of water at an unknown temperature are placed in an insulated bag. Upon reaching thermal equilibrium, the half-full bottle’s temperature increases to $20\degree\text{C}$. The specific heat of water is $4.18\,\dfrac{\text{kJ}}{\text{kg}\degree\text{C}}$. Assume the system is thermally isolated.

**Which of the following statements is correct?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: To solve this problem, we need to calculate the change in thermal energy. To do this, we can use the formula $Q = mC( T_{\text{f}} -T_{\text{i}} )$, where $m$ is the mass of the object, $C$ is its specific heat capacity, $T_\text{f}$ and $T_\text{i}$ are its final and initial temperatures respectively, and $Q$ is the change in thermal energy.

We don’t know the initial temperature of the full bottle. But since the system is thermally isolated, the total thermal energy of the system does not change. Therefore, the energy that the half-full bottle gained was transferred from the full bottle. So, the full bottle must have lost $62.7\text{ kJ}$.
So, the half-full bottle gained $62.7\text{ kJ}$ of thermal energy, and the full bottle lost $62.7\text{ kJ}$ of thermal energy.

choices.content: The half-full bottle gained $62.7\text{ kJ}$ of thermal energy, and the full bottle lost $62.7\text{ kJ}$ of thermal energy.
The half-full bottle lost $62.7\text{ kJ}$ of thermal energy, and the full bottle gained $62.7\text{ kJ}$ of thermal energy.
The half-full bottle gained $62.7\text{ kJ}$ of thermal energy, and the full bottle lost $125.4\text{ kJ}$ of thermal energy.
The half-full bottle lost $62.7\text{ kJ}$ of thermal energy, and the full bottle gained $125.4\text{ kJ}$ of thermal energy.

assessment_items.question_content:
A student has a bottle of orange juice that is almost $\dfrac{1}{3}$ full and at $24\degree\text{C}$. He refills it from a pitcher from the fridge that is at $6\degree\text{C}$. Assume that the juice bottle is thermally isolated.

**Which of the following would be the most likely final temperature of the juice bottle?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: When the orange juice from the pitcher is mixed with the orange juice in the bottle, the two liquids will reach thermal equilibrium. So, we can compare their thermal energies to estimate the final temperature of the system.

Because the system is thermally isolated, we know that no energy is lost or gained and that the final temperature must be in between the two initial temperatures. So, $5\degree\text{C}$ and $30\degree\text{C}$ are not possible answers.

The juice in the bottle has the same specific heat capacity and phase as the juice from the pitcher. So, the masses and initial temperatures of the juices determine the amount and direction of heat transfer.  

The bottle is almost $\dfrac{1}{3}$ full to start, so we know that the mass of the liquid poured from the pitcher is greater than the mass of the liquid from the bottle. Because the liquid at $6\degree\text{C}$ has more mass, it takes more energy to raise its temperature. Because of this, the final temperature must be closer to $6\degree\text{C}$ than to $24\degree\text{C}$. Since $12\degree\text{C}$ is closer to $6\degree\text{C}$ than $22\degree\text{C}$, $12\degree\text{C}$ is the best estimate of the final temperature of the orange juice.
Therefore, the most likely final temperature of the juice bottle is $12\degree\text{C}$.

choices.content: $5\degree\text{C}$
$12\degree\text{C}$
$22\degree\text{C}$
$30\degree\text{C}$

assessment_items.question_content:
A trucker has a thermos that is $\dfrac{1}{4}$ full with coffee at $40\degree\text{C}$. He completely fills the thermos with coffee at $80\degree\text{C}$. Assume that the thermos is thermally isolated. 

**Which of the following would be the most likely final temperature of the coffee in the thermos?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: When the coffee from the thermos is mixed with the new coffee, the two liquids will reach thermal equilibrium. So, we can compare their thermal energies to estimate the final temperature of the system.

Because the system is thermally isolated, we know that no energy is lost or gained and that the final temperature must be in between the two initial temperatures. So, $40\degree\text{C}$ and $90\degree\text{C}$ are not possible answers.

The coffee in the thermos has the same specific heat capacity and phase as the fresh coffee. So, the masses and initial temperatures of the different amounts of coffee determine the amount and direction of heat transfer.  

The thermos is $\dfrac{1}{4}$ full to start, so we know that the mass of the fresh coffee added to the thermos is greater than the mass of the coffee that starts in the thermos. Because the coffee at $80\degree\text{C}$ has more mass, it has to lose more energy to lower its temperature. Because of this, the final temperature must be closer to $80\degree\text{C}$ than to $40\degree\text{C}$. Since $70\degree\text{C}$ is closer to $80\degree\text{C}$ than $55\degree\text{C}$, $70\degree\text{C}$ is the best estimate of the final temperature of the coffee.
Therefore, the most likely final temperature of the coffee in the thermos is $70\degree\text{C}$.

choices.content: $40\degree\text{C}$
$55\degree\text{C}$
$70\degree\text{C}$
$90\degree\text{C}$

assessment_items.question_content:
A fisherman has a thermos that is $\dfrac{2}{3}$ full with coffee at $45\degree\text{C}$. He completely fills the thermos with coffee at $90\degree\text{C}$. Assume that the thermos is thermally isolated. 

**Which of the following would be the most likely final temperature of the coffee in the thermos?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: When the coffee from the thermos is mixed with the new coffee, the two liquids will reach thermal equilibrium. So, we can compare their thermal energies to estimate the final temperature of the system.

Because the system is thermally isolated, we know that no energy is lost or gained and that the final temperature must be in between the two initial temperatures. So, $45\degree\text{C}$ and $90\degree\text{C}$ are not possible answers.

The coffee in the thermos has the same specific heat capacity and phase as the fresh coffee. So, the masses and initial temperatures of the different amounts of coffee determine the amount and direction of heat transfer.  

The thermos is $\dfrac{2}{3}$ full to start, so we know that the mass of the fresh coffee in the thermos is greater than the mass of the coffee added to the thermos. Because the coffee at $45\degree\text{C}$ has more mass, it takes more energy to raise its temperature. Because of this, the final temperature must be closer to $45\degree\text{C}$ than to $90\degree\text{C}$. Since $60\degree\text{C}$ is closer to $45\degree\text{C}$ than $85\degree\text{C}$, $60\degree\text{C}$ is the best estimate of the final temperature of the coffee.
Therefore, the most likely final temperature of the coffee in the thermos is $60\degree\text{C}$.

choices.content: $45\degree\text{C}$
$60\degree\text{C}$
$85\degree\text{C}$
$90\degree\text{C}$
